coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of biomakers for biological warfare agents with carbon dots chelated europium-based nanoscale coordination polymers
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yonghai
Chen, Jingyi
Hu, Dongqin
Liu, Fenfen
Li, Ping
Li, Hongbo
Chen, Shouhui
Tan, Hongliang
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for biological warfare agent 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) detection is developed with carbon dots (CDs)@europium-based nanoscale coordination polymers (Eu-NCPs). The abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of CDs are served as starting points for the spontaneous self-assembly of europium ion (Eu3+) and 5′-guanosine monophosphate disodium (GMP) on the surface of CDs to form CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites. The obtained nanosensor exhibits the characteristic emission of CDs and weak emission of Eu3+. The Eu/GMP NCPs are served as the DPA recognition sites. The fluorescence of CDs remains constant as a reference, while the fluorescence of Eu/GMP NCPs is enhanced upon binding with DPA, resulting in ratiometric fluorescent response toward DPA. This ratiometric nanosensor exhibits good selectivity toward DPA detection. The ratio of F 615/F 469 linearly increases with the increasing of DPA concentration in the range of 25nM–5.0μM with a detection limit as low as 5.1nM. Additionally, the nanosensor is successfully applied for the determination of DPA in the real serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
Carbon dots
5′-Guanosine monophosphate disodium
2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid
Europium-based nanoscale coordination polymers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance of CDs@Eu/GMP as nanosensor toward DPA detection

The Ministry of Education by the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education ( 20133604110002 ), the Ground Plan of Science and Technology Projects of Jiangxi Educational Committee ( KJLD14023 ) and the Open Project Program of Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Jiangxi Normal University (No. KLFS-KF-201410 ; KLFS-KF-201416 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic route for CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites and mechanism for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of DPA.
(A) AFM image of CDs and inset: size distribution of the CDs. (B) SEM image of CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites. (C, D) Differently magnified TEM images of CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites.

(A) UV–vis spectra of CDs (black), CDs@Eu/GMP (blue) and CDs@Eu/GMP-DPA (red). (B) FTIR spectra of CDs (blue), CDs@Eu/GMP (red) and CDs@Eu/GMP-DPA (purple). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@Eu/GMP nanosensor exposed to various concentrations of DPA: 0.025, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0μM (pH=7, 50mM Tris–HCl buffer). (B) Plot of F 615/F 469 versus the concentration of DPA. The concentration of CDs@Eu/GMP 50mgml−1. The excitation wavelength is 275nm.

Selectivity test of CDs@Eu/GMP (50mgml−1) for DPA detection over several aromatic ligands and amino acids (pH=7, 50mM Tris–HCl buffer). The concentrations of all the compounds are the same (5.0μM) and measurements are performed under identical conditions.
Comparison of sensing performance of different fluorescent probe for DPA detection.

row.entry: Fluorescein isothiocyanate dye doped silica nanoparticles coordinate with Eu-EDTA
SiO2 nanoparticles coordinate with Tb-EDTA b

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Dye-loadded zeolite L@silica core–shell composite functionalized with europium (III) complexes.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of biomakers for biological warfare agents with carbon dots chelated europium-based nanoscale coordination polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology, Jiangxi Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang 330022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology, Jiangxi Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for biological warfare agent 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) detection is developed with carbon dots (CDs)@europium-based nanoscale coordination polymers (Eu-NCPs). The abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of CDs are served as starting points for the spontaneous self-assembly of europium ion (Eu3+) and 5′-guanosine monophosphate disodium (GMP) on the surface of CDs to form CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites. The obtained nanosensor exhibits the characteristic emission of CDs and weak emission of Eu3+. The Eu/GMP NCPs are served as the DPA recognition sites. The fluorescence of CDs remains constant as a reference, while the fluorescence of Eu/GMP NCPs is enhanced upon binding with DPA, resulting in ratiometric fluorescent response toward DPA. This ratiometric nanosensor exhibits good selectivity toward DPA detection. The ratio of F 615/F 469 linearly increases with the increasing of DPA concentration in the range of 25nM–5.0μM with a detection limit as low as 5.1nM. Additionally, the nanosensor is successfully applied for the determination of DPA in the real serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoscale coordination polymers (NCPs), constructed from metal ions and organic bridging ligands, have attracted great attention owing to their diverse compositions and architectures, high loading capacity, structural and chemical diversity and intrinsic biodegradability, especially their luminescent properties [1–7]. The luminescent properties of NCPs can be easily tailored by changing the ligands, lumophore type, metal ion and spatial arrangements for particular applications [8–11]. Their specific recognition ability together with their luminescent properties have made them been extensively used as fluorescent probes to monitor ions and small molecules, such as Ag+, aromatic amines, explosives, etc. [12–17]. However, almost all these assays of NCPs-based sensors are carried out in organic solution based on the change of fluorescence intensity, which will deserve serious interference from drift of light resource or detector, the concentration change of probes especially at very low analyte's concentration and environmental factors [18–21]. The problems can be solved by using ratiometric fluorescent detection based on the relative changes of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths [22,23].

The 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acids (DPA) are major components in protective layers of bacterial spores, about 5–15% of the dry mass of bacterial spores [24,25]. Bacillus anthracis is concurrently a potential biological warfare agent to cause the disease Anthrax and an inhalation of more than 104 B. anthracis spores can lead to death [26–28]. Therefore, the rapid, sensitive, accurate and selective detection of bacterial spores is of great importance to prevent a biological attack of disease. Methods such as high-pressure liquid chromatography, electrochemical detection, surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), polymerase chain reactions (PCR), immunoassays and fluorescence have been used to detect DPA [29–38]. For example, Li and Wang have designed a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor constructed by fluorescence dyes chelating with lanthanide ion (Ln3+) for DPA detection [32,33,37]. However, lanthanide-based fluorescent detection is limited to molecular lanthanide compounds or inorganic nanomaterials and their luminescent properties are not easy to be controlled. Fortunately, the NCPs can make up the shortages of traditional Ln3+-based materials. As discussed above, luminescent properties of NCPs can be regulated by organic bridging ligands or ion, thus providing new promising strategy for DPA detection. Besides, most of ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor use fluorescence dyes to serve as references. However, fluorescence dyes easily suffer from photobleaching and are toxicant to living organism. To overcome the shortages, quantum dots (QDs) especially carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great interest due to their good photostability, low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility [39–52]. Therefore, the incorporation of the reference CDs as a non-interfering internal calibration is beneficial for ratiometric fluorescent sensor for DPA detection.

Herein, a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for the detection of the Bacillus anthracis biomarker DPA with high sensitivity and selectivity based on CDs@Eu-NCPs was proposed for the first time. The CDs were firstly synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan and could emit fluorescence under different excitation wavelength owing to its abundant functional groups such as hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups. The Eu3+ and 5′-guanosine monophosphate disodium (5′-GMP) were subsequently assembled on CDs’ surface to form CDs@Eu/GMP NCPs [53,54]. The obtained nanosensor exhibited the characteristic emission of CDs as a reference and weak emission of Eu3+ as probes. The ratiometric nanosensor based on CDs@Eu/GMP NCPs exhibited very good performance toward DPA detection, which was investigated in detail.

Europium nitrate (Eu(NO3)3, 99.99%) was purchased from Rewin Rare Earth Metal Materials Co., Ltd (Baotou, China). The DPA was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. GMP, 2-amino-2-(hydroxymethyl)-1,3-propanediol (Tris), p-phthalic acid, benzoic acid, trimestic acid, l-asparaginic acid, l-glutamic acid and l-cysteine were purchased from Sangon (Shanghai, China). Acetic acid and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co. Ltd. (Guangdong, China). All solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water, purified by a Millipore-Q system (18.2MΩcm−1). Tris–HCl buffer (50mM, pH=7) was prepared by dissolving appropriate amounts of Tris in ultra-pure water and adjusting the pH with 1.0M HCl. All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The 5.0μL fetal calf serum was diluted 1000-fold with 50mM Tris–HCl buffer (pH=7) with a final concentration of 0.1% (v/v) serum. A series of serum samples containing different concentrations of DPA in the range of 0–5.0μM were prepared by spiking different volumes of DPA (50μM). Then the probe containing 10μL CDs@Eu/GMP (1.2mgmL−1) was added into serum samples and the final volume was 100μL. After reaction for 10min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

The CDs were prepared by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan [40]. Briefly, 1.0g chitosan was dissolved in 9.0mL 2.0% acetic acid in a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave at 180°C for 12h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained dark brown solution was centrifuged at a speed of 13,000rpm for 30min to remove off the less-fluorescent precipitates. The upper brown solution was then filtered with a 0.22μm filter membrane to obtain the CDs.

To prepare the CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites, 4.0mL 10mM GMP aqueous solution was firstly mixed with 500μL CDs (400mgL−1) and stirred for 30min. Then 4.0mL 10mM Eu(NO3)3 was added into the mixed solution and stirred for 2h at room temperature. Next, the products were collected by centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 5min and washing with ultra-pure water for three times to remove off unreacted reagent. Last, the solid products were dried in vacuum at 60°C for 24h.

The CDs@Eu/GMP suspension was prepared by dispersing 6.0mg CDs@Eu/GMP powder in 5.0mL ultra-pure water under ultrasonication for 10min. For quantization of DPA concentration, 80μL Tris–HCl buffer (pH 7, 50mM) was added into 10μL CDs@Eu/GMP (1.2mgmL−1), and then 10μL DPA in the range of 0–50μM were added to the above solution. The final volume of the reaction solution was adjusted to 100μL. The final concentration of CDs@Eu/GMP was 0.12mgmL−1 and the concentration of DPA in the reaction solutions were in the range of 0–5.0μM. For interference experiments, the 10μL 50μM p-phthalic acid, benzoic acid, trimestic acid, l-asparaginic acid, l-glutamic acid, l-cysteine were added to CDs@Eu/GMP solution (1.2mgmL−1), respectively. The final concentrations of these substances were 5.0μM. Finally, the emission spectra at 275nm excitation of these reaction solutions were recorded.

The measurements of fluorescence spectra were performed on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The emission spectra were monitored using a 275nm excitation wavelength. Lambda 35 spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, U.K.) was employed to record the UV–vis absorption spectra. Avatar 360 Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) spectrometer (Nicolet, USA) was applied to record FTIR spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out on a TEM-20f10 (HR) microscope. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were carried out with an AJ-III AFM system (Shanghai Aijian Nanotechnology) in tapping mode. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image was taken using a XL30 ESEM-FEG SEM at an accelerating voltage of 20kV equipped with a Phoenix energy dispersive X-ray analyzer.

The AFM image of CDs showed that the particles were spherical nanoparticles with a diameter of 1.4nm (inset in Fig. 1A) and uniformly dispersed on mica surface, indicating CDs could be well dispersed in aqueous solution. The SEM image of CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites showed that the CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites were composed of nanofibers which crosslinked each other to form networks on nanoscale dimensions (Fig. 1B). The result was further confirmed by the low-magnification TEM image in Fig. 1C. High-magnification TEM image clearly exhibited a large number of CDs in the networks as marked by arrow, indicating CDs was successfully encapsulated into the Eu/GMP networks (Fig. 1D).

In order to further confirm the successful synthesis of CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites and the coordination interaction between DPA and CDs@Eu/GMP, UV–vis absorption spectra and FTIR spectra of CDs, CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites and CDs@Eu/GMP-PDA nanocomposites were performed. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs showed a strong absorption peak at 292nm, arising from functional groups on the surface of CDs (Fig. 2A). As for CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites, a strong absorption peak at 253nm and a shoulder peak from 267nm to 305nm could be assigned to the absorption of GMP. As the absorption of CDs was overlapped with the shoulder absorption of CDs@Eu/GMP, the characteristic peak of CDs at 292nm was hidden at shoulder peak for CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites. After the addition of DPA, the characteristic peak of DPA at 270nm and 276nm was observed, indicating the binding of DPA with CDs@Eu/GMP.

FTIR spectra of CDs, CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites and CDs@Eu/GMP-PDA nanocomposites were shown in Fig. 2B. FTIR spectra of CDs showed that a broad OH stretching vibration at 3337cm−1 accompanying with CO absorption at 1080cm−1, indicating the presence of OH in CDs. The sharp band at 1750cm−1 indicated the presence of CO bonds. Besides, the absorption at 1649cm−1 was ascribed to the deformational vibration of NH2, suggesting the presence of abundant NH2 group on the surface of CDs [41]. The abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs were essential for the coordination with Eu/GMP, as well as improving the solubility and reducing the potential biotoxicity for the following biological application. As for CDs@Eu/GMP, the peaks at about 3337cm−1, 1649cm−1 and 1080cm−1 were weaken and the peak at 1750cm−1 disappeared, indicating the CO group, NH2 and OH groups on the surface of CDs interacted with Eu/GMP. Besides, the characteristic peak of PO4 3− and NH of GMP appeared at 1084cm−1 and 1540cm−1, respectively, suggesting the successful synthesis of CDs@Eu/GMP. Upon addition of DPA, the peaks at 2367cm−1 and 1389cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of CN of DPA were observed, while the characteristic peaks of GMP remained unchanged, indicating DPA was coordinated with Eu3+.

The preparation and sensing mechanism of ratiometric nanosensor were shown in Scheme 1 . Fluorescent CDs were firstly synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan. And then Eu3+ was accumulated on CDs by chelating with surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which was used as starting points for self-assembly of Eu3+ and GMP to form Eu/GMP around CDs. The obtained nanosensor showed one strong emission peak at 469nm originated from CDs emission and three weak emission peaks at 592nm, 615nm and 694nm from characteristic emission of Eu3+ under the excitation of 275nm (Fig. S1, Supporting information). It was well known that the fluorescent properties of CDs were dependent on their sizes and the excitation wavelength [55]. The fluorescence spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelength were shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting information). As could be seen in Fig. S2 (Supporting information), the CDs showed strong blue emission in 260–340nm. However, with further increasing of the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence intensity greatly decreased with the red-shifted emission. Such behaviors could be ascribed to the abundant functional groups which acted as excitation energy traps to result in different fluorescent properties. Under the excitation of 275nm, the 1.4nm-sized CDs gave the moderate intensity of blue-emitting which could well match and distinguish from the red emission of Eu3+. Thus, the excitation of 275nm was used for ratiometric fluorescence detection. The Eu3+ excited state could couple with the high frequency OH oscillators of H2O, resulting in the weak fluorescence of Eu/GMP. Upon the addition of DPA, O in carboxylate and N in aromate ring of DPA could strongly coordinate with Eu3+ to completely replace H2O from Eu3+ center, resulting in the enhanced fluorescence of Eu/GMP. However, no change was observed for the fluorescence of CDs, even at high concentration of DPA. Therefore, CDs was suitable to act as reference upon excitation at proper excitation wavelength and the hydrophilic CDs@Eu/GMP nanosensor could be successfully used as fluorescence probe for ratiometric detection of DPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CDs@Eu/GMP as nanosensor toward DPA detection
ce:section.ce:para: To gain a high sensitivity and low detection limit for DPA detection, the condition for the sensing of DPA was optimized. Sensors based on the changes of fluorescence intensity were susceptible to test conditions such as environmental pH, sensor concentration, excitation wavelength, etc. The errors originated from the test conditions could be overcome by a ratiometric strategy which allowed for a built-in correction to overcome the errors come from environments effects, especially the variations in sensors concentration [56–58]. So only the response time was explored. As shown in Fig. S3 (Supporting Information), in the absence of DPA, the CDs@Eu/GMP exhibited no observable changes in the ratio of emission intensities at 615nm and 469nm (F 615/F 469 =0.401), indicating the nanosensor was quite stable. As a comparison, upon addition of DPA at room temperature, a pronounced enhancement of F 615/F 469 was obtained even after 2min, indicative of a rapid coordination between DPA and Eu3+. After about 10min, the F 615/F 469 arrived at maximum value. Thus, 10min was chosen to further examine the sensitivity and selectivity of the nanosensor toward DPA detection in the following experiments.

To evaluate the performance of CDs@Eu/GMP as nanosensor toward DPA detection, the DPA with different concentrations were added into the CDs@Eu/GMP solution to measure the fluorescence responses under the above-mentioned optimum experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ emission at 615nm was very sensitive and increased gradually with the increasing concentration of DPA, revealing that DPA strongly coordinated with the Eu3+ center to replace H2O from the Eu3+ [59]. The F 615/F 469 was linearly increased with the concentration of DPA in the range of 25nM–5.0μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9963 (Fig. 3B). The detection limit was calculated to be 5.1nM based on the ratio of signal-to-noise of 3, which was much lower than the maximum concentration of DPA (about 0.1μM) for human being [60].

The result was comparable to that of the previously reported lanthanide-based fluorescent sensors or ratiometric fluorescent sensors (Table 1 ) [26,32–37,61]. Taking automated anthrax smoke detector as an example, the detection limit was lower (0.2nM) than that of the CDs@Eu/GMP, but the linear range was narrower (10–100nM) [26]. For SiO2 nanoparticles coordinated with Tb-EDTA, the linear range was wider (0–1200nM), but the detection limit was higher (10.3nM) [36]. By comparing, it could be clearly seen that the linear range was wider than that of sensors, and the detection limit was lower. What is more important, the CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites could directly detect DPA in aqueous solutions, while some sensors mentioned in Table 1 have to be dispersed in organic solution. Therefore, CDs@Eu/GMP showed some obvious advantages in the sensing performance. The good performance of this nanosensor might be ascribed to the CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites and the ratiometric sensing of DPA. The Eu-NCPs not only provided the greatest possible surface area for sensing of DPA, but also significantly minimized the nonradiative quenching of Eu emission, thus offering a corresponding improvement in the detection sensitivity for DPA detection [33]. Additionally, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor could eliminate environmental effects arising from fluctuation in probe concentration and instrument efficiency, thereby give more precise measurements.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of CDs@Eu/GMP as a sensing platform for DPA, the changes of F 615/F 469 in the presence of different aromatic ligands or amino acid such as p-phthalic acid, benzoic acid, trimestic acid, l-asparaginic acid, l-glutamic acid, l-cysteine acid were investigated in 5.0μM Tris–HCl buffer at pH 7 (Fig. 4 ). The small or negligible changes of F 615/F 469 indicated the excellent selectivity for DPA detection. The high selectivity might be ascribed to the fact that O in carboxylate and N in aromate ring of DPA could strongly coordinate with Eu3+ to completely replace water molecules from Eu3+ center. While other aromate ligands could not displace all the coordinating water molecules and the residual water molecules could still quench Eu3+ emission of the sensor [33].

The practicability of the CDs@Eu/GMP nanocomposites was also assessed by determining DPA in fetal calf serum samples. The spiked fetal calf serum samples were obtained by adding different amounts of DPA to the diluted serum. The added standard DPA could be measured with good recoveries and small relative standard deviations (RSD) (Table S1, Supporting information), suggesting the ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor was accurate and credible.

In summary, a ratiometric fluorescence nanosenor based on CDs@Eu/GMP NCPs for DPA detection has been designed through encapsulating CDs in Eu/GMP. The as-prepared nanosensor exhibited the characteristic emission spectra of CDs as reference and weak emission of Eu3+ as recognition sites for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of DPA in aqueous solutions. The selectivity assay demonstrated that the nanosensor had good selectivity to DPA over other aromatic ligands. The CDs@Eu/GMP nanosensor exhibited a good linear response in the range of 25nM–5.0μM with a detection limit of 5.1nM. This strategy is expected to be extended to the construction of other coordination polymer nanocomposites for the potential application of fluorescence imaging of DPA in cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21165010, 21465014 and 21465015), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20142BAB203101 and 20143ACB21016). The Ministry of Education by the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20133604110002), the Ground Plan of Science and Technology Projects of Jiangxi Educational Committee (KJLD14023) and the Open Project Program of Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Jiangxi Normal University (No. KLFS-KF-201410; KLFS-KF-201416).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.07.008.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

Determination of tetracycline in milk by using nucleotide/lanthanide coordination polymer-based ternary complex
Fluorescent metal–organic framework for selective sensing of nitroaromatic explosives
Ag+-enhanced fluorescence of lanthanide/nucleotide coordination polymers and Ag+ sensing
A microporous luminescent metal–organic framework for highly selective and sensitive sensing of Cu2+ in aqueous solution
Luminescent photoinduced electron transfer (PET) molecules for sensing and logic operations
A luminescent nanocrystal metal–organic framework for sensing of nitroaromatic compounds
A luminescent nanoscale metal–organic framework for sensing of nitroaromatic explosives
Facile and rapid fabrication of nanostructured lanthanide coordination polymers as selective luminescent probes in aqueous solution
ZnS:Mn nanoparticles functionalized by PAMAM–OH dendrimer based fluorescence ratiometric probe for cadmium
A highly selective wavelength ratiometric and colorimetric probe for cysteine
Acylhydrazone based fluorescent chemosensor for zinc in aqueous solution with high selectivity and sensitivity
Carbon-dot-based dual-emission nanohybrid produces a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for in vivo imaging of cellular copper ions
A ratiometric fluorescent probe for magnesium employing excited state intramolecular proton transfer
Development of a holographic sensor for the detection of calcium dipicolinate – a sensitive biomarker for bacterial spores
An automated front-end monitor for anthrax surveillance systems based on the rapid detection of airborne endospores
Explosive and chemical threat detection by surface-enhanced Raman scattering: a review
Rapid detection of an anthrax biomarker by surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy
Real-time PCR assay for a unique chromosomal sequence of Bacillus anthracis
Development of a rapid and sensitive immunoassay for detection and subsequent recovery of Bacillus anthracis spores in environmental samples
Ratiometric luminescent detection of bacterial spores with terbium chelated semiconducting polymer dots
Europium-based fluorescence nanoparticle sensor for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of an anthrax biomarker
Lanthanide-functionalized silver nanoparticles for detection of an anthrax biomarker and test paper fabrication
A luminescent nanoscale metal–organic framework with controllable morphologies for spore detection
Hybrid silica nanoparticles for luminescent spore detection
Dye-loaded zeolite@silica core–shell composite functionalized with europium (III) complexes for dipicolinic acid detection
Fluorescent europium-modified polymer nanoparticles for rapid and sensitive anthrax sensors
Simple aqueous solution route to luminescent carbogenic dots from carbohydrates
One-step synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan
Growth and stabilization of silver nanoparticles on carbon dots and sensing application
CdS quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for nucleic acid detection

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost, green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu (II) ions
Production of superoxide anion radicals as evidence for carbon nanodots acting as electron donors by the chemiluminescence method
Classical oxidant induced chemiluminescence of fluorescent carbon dots
Novel design of biped robot using cable differential joint

Submicrometre-scale polyaniline colloidal spheres: photopolymerization preparation using fluorescent carbon nitride dots as a photocatalyst
Preparation of photoluminescent carbon nitride dots from CCl4 and 1,2-ethylenediamine: a heat-treatment-based strategy
Microwave-assisted rapid green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots from flour and their applications for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Carbon nitride dots can serve as an effective stabilizing agent for reduced graphene oxide and help in subsequent assembly with glucose oxidase into hybrids for glucose detection application
Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Selective inclusion of anionic quantum dots in coordination network shells of nucleotides and lanthanide ions
Encapsulation of strongly fluorescent carbon quantum dots in metal–organic frameworks for enhancing chemical sensing
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
From pair to single: sole fluorophore for ratiometric sensing by dual-emitting quantum dots
Intracellular cyclic AMP not calcium, determines the direction of vesicle movement in melanophores: direct measurement by fluorescence ratio imaging
Ratiometric activatable cell-penetrating peptides provide rapid in vivo readout of thrombin activation
Noncytotoxic, bifunctional Eu(III) and Tb(III) luminescent macrocyclic complexes for luminescence resonant energy-transfer experiments
Ultraviolet-absorption spectra of dry bacterial spores
Surface modification and functionalization of nanoscale metal–organic frameworks for controlled release and luminescence sensing

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yonghai Song received his Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science, China. He is currently working as a professor at Jiangxi Normal University. His current research interests focus on biosensors.

Jingyi Chen received her science bachelor in chemistry in 2013 from Jiangxi Normal University, China. She is working for her master's degree in Jiangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests are nano-materials and their applications in sensor.

Dongqin Hu received her science bachelor in chemistry in 2012 from Gannan Normal University, China. She is working for her master's degree in Jiangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests are nano-materials and their applications in sensor.

Fenfen Liu received her science bachelor in chemistry in 2013 from Jiangxi Normal University, China. She is working for her master's degree in Jiangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests are nano-materials and their applications in sensor.
Ping Li received her master degree in 2004 from University of Science and Technology of China. He is working as a lecturer in Jiangxi Normal University, China. His research interest is biosensors.
Hongbo Li received his Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from Hunan University, China. He is working as a lecturer in Jiangxi Normal University, China. His research interest is biosensors.

Shouhui Chen received his master degree from Nanchang University, China. He is working as a lecturer in Jiangxi Normal University, China. Her research interests are nano-materials and their applications in sensor.

Hongliang Tan received his Ph.D. from Southeast University, China. He is currently working as an associate professor at Jiangxi Normal University. His current research interest is focused on fluorescence sensor.

Li Wang received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science, China. She is currently working as a professor at Jiangxi Normal University. Her current research interest is focused on biosensors.
